board.verdict: The mineral instrument is deployed. On June 22, Beijing's Ministry of Commerce placed ten US entities — including MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — on its export control list. On June 25, Beijing simultaneously confirmed it will continue tariff reduction discussions with Washington. The desk reads these as one architecture: the monopoly creates the leverage; the negotiation is where the leverage generates returns. The fourth CISCE closed June 26 with US companies as the largest foreign group for the fourth consecutive year; Apple and NVIDIA exhibited alongside Chinese supply chain partners. The Burgenstock framework produced the seating-chart signal: Pakistan, Qatar, Switzerland — no European power. Now the table has migrated to Doha: the June 30 technical session, originally scheduled for Switzerland, moved after mutual strikes around the Strait of Hormuz. The topic shifted from nuclear to maritime. The Atlantic order's Lebanon brokerage — Washington framework carrying a Hezbollah disarmament condition that Hezbollah's parliament ally rejected the same day it was announced — has produced another document without compliance. Burkina Faso's severance of French diplomatic ties on June 26, missed by this desk Thursday, is multipolarity legible inside France's own policy space: IMF disbursement, France expulsion, and AES UNGA coordination in a single week.
board.watching: Iran Doha session June 30 — Hormuz de-escalation or walkout; Lebanon IDF operations as Iran walkout trigger; France reciprocal measures against Burkina Faso — content and whether AES can absorb them; Tumen bridge Russia completion (minimum another year per Daily NK June 24); BRICS Bridge framework at India summit.

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Then, on June 17, the PBOC announced the FIMA RMB repo facility: overseas central banks can now access yuan the way they have always accessed dollars — frictionlessly, through collateral, without onshore accounts. The Fed built this mechanism for dollars in 2021; Beijing has now copied it in yuan. The adversary will call this marginal. The desk calls it the one structural advantage dollars held that is now being systematically replicated. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged; the pace just accelerated.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER; foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility.

sources.label: Crypto Briefing — China FIMA RMB repo facility, June 17
The Block — mBridge, BIS context

board.verdict: A correction is required on the Tumen bridge. The desk filed June 19 that the Khasan-Tumangang road bridge had opened. Daily NK reported June 24, citing officials in Rason, that the bridge is not operational: Russia did not complete its connecting road infrastructure and customs facilities on its side before the June 19 target date, and officials in Rason estimate the delay at a minimum of another year. North Korea completed its side on schedule — customs house, bridge deck, asphalt paving, all finished. The bridge sits idle. The desk has been running a 'no cargo data' thread since June 19 with no explanation; that thread now has its explanation. The direction of travel (Russia-DPRK partnership deepening) is unchanged; the timeline is not. What was filed as an opening was a ceremony. Pyongyang fulfilled its obligations; Moscow did not — creating a creditor position Pyongyang may use to extract economic concessions or to crowd Chinese logistical leverage. The Choe Hyon destroyer was commissioned June 23 and remains in service; the WPK plenary's nuclear expansion mandate stands. June 28: thin day — no fresh DPRK news in 48 hours. Beijing's silence on Kim's naval program and nuclear posture continues unbroken.
board.watching: Russia's revised completion timeline for bridge approach roads and customs facilities; whether Pyongyang's frustration surfaces in bilateral channels; whether Beijing's continued silence on the Choe Hyon, WPK plenary nuclear posture, and Russia-DPRK second anniversary (June 19) continues through the India BRICS summit.

board.verdict: The IMF Executive Board voted June 26. The $104.89 million disbursement is approved: Fifth ECF Review completion with 50-percent augmentation (SDR 60.20 million, approximately $82 million immediate disbursement) plus First RSF Review (SDR 16.42 million, approximately $22 million). The Board recorded 5.3 percent economic growth in 2025, a current-account moving toward surplus, fiscal deficit reduced from 5.8 percent of GDP in 2024 to 1.8 percent in 2025. Ibrahim Traoré has publicly rejected IMF and World Bank 'aid' throughout this period. The desk's read: this is not aid. It is credit against performance criteria that Ouagadougou has, by the Board's own account, met. The gold revenue bought the fiscal space; the fiscal space bought the performance metrics; the performance metrics bought the disbursement. On the same day, June 26, Burkina Faso severed diplomatic relations with France — the most significant Sahel geopolitical event of the month, missed by this desk Thursday. The government accused Paris of 'blatant neo-colonial ambitions and active support for subversive networks and terrorists destabilizing our country and the Sahel.' France called it 'hostile and unfounded' and announced reciprocal measures under review; as of Saturday, those measures had not been specified. The break completes a logic running since 2022: ambassador expelled 2023, three diplomats expelled 2024, France 24 banned, now the full rupture. The AES foreign ministers had met in Bamako on June 20 and adopted a common diplomatic roadmap for the September 2026 UN General Assembly — harmonized positions, 'diversified' partners. Read the three documents from the same week together: IMF disbursement, France severance, UNGA coordination. They are one policy at three registers. The two registers — sovereignty rhetoric and IMF facility mechanics — are not in contradiction. They are a single policy operating on two audiences simultaneously.
board.watching: France reciprocal measures — content and whether they include anything Ouagadougou cannot absorb without Russian or Chinese bridging support; AES UNGA September 2026 common position; gold revenue allocation to unified security force; AES trade roadmap with concrete figures.

board.verdict: On June 22, Beijing's MOFCOM deployed the mineral instrument: ten US entities banned from receiving Chinese dual-use exports, including MP Materials (the only active rare earth mine in the US) and USA Rare Earth. On June 25, Beijing continued tariff board discussions with Washington. These operate as a single leverage architecture, not contradictory impulses — the monopoly makes the negotiation worth having. No new domestic NBS data today. The structural read is unchanged: the July Politburo is where the domestic demand problem gets or does not get a fiscal response. The desk's June 16 prediction (June retail sales also negative) runs until mid-July. The standing FAI prediction (exits contraction by Q4 2026) has not been falsified; the May figure (FAI -4.1% Jan-May) requires honest accounting: the contraction is deepening, not stabilizing.
board.watching: July Politburo response to May NBS; June retail sales (released mid-July) for June 16 prediction resolution; whether any targeted stimulus appears before the July Politburo date; whether US critical minerals legislation response to MOFCOM export controls accelerates; semiconductor export growth trajectory.

board.verdict: The technical session scheduled for Geneva was cancelled after an exchange of strikes in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Iran and the United States agreed to halt attacks and will meet instead on Tuesday in Doha — the topic now Hormuz de-escalation, not the nuclear chapter. Iran's FM Araghchi on June 29: 'responsibility for the Strait of Hormuz lies solely with Tehran.' Commercial shipping at five percent of pre-crisis volume. An Iranian drone struck Qatar on June 28; a Qatari citizen died from shrapnel. Meanwhile, the Washington framework on Lebanon — June 26 document tying IDF withdrawal to prior Hezbollah disarmament — was rejected by Lebanon's Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri on June 29: 'will not pass,' 'incitement to civil war.' Hezbollah declared it 'null and void.' IDF operations in the security zone continued Sunday, including dismantling a 200-meter tunnel complex. The deconfliction cell does not govern IDF operational authority. The structure is now explicit: the Atlantic order can produce compliance documents; it cannot produce compliance. The Lebanon fuse remains live. The desk's June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) and June 24 prediction (July 13 IAEA benchmark disputed rather than cleanly met) both run with increasing force.
board.watching: Iran Doha session June 30 — Hormuz de-escalation language or Iranian walkout; Lebanon IDF operations as Iran walkout trigger — Berri has already declared no; June 15 prediction: no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026; June 24 prediction: July 13 IAEA benchmark dispute rather than clean access.

wars.basis: Ukraine launched its largest drone campaign of the war — 660-plus systems in a single night, June 28-29 — including cruise missiles against the Titan-Barrikady armaments plant in Volgograd, a producer of rocket and artillery systems, and fires at the Slavyansk-na-Kubani refinery (Krasnodar Krai) and the Yaroslavl complex. Moscow claimed 200-plus intercepts; industrial fires resulted. The Titan-Barrikady strike is material: it targets production capacity. Whether it interrupts output is the question the desk will track. The adversary framing (NPR: 'reversed momentum'; CFR: drone innovation decisive) is intelligence about what Washington needs to believe, not operational analysis. On the same night, 197 ground combat engagements were logged with the highest density in the Sloviansk sector. Prior: Russian forces took Nykyforivka and Lypivka southeast of Sloviansk on June 27. The Yuzhnaya Group's contact phase at Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka continues; the desk's September 1 prediction (operational control before Sept 1) is in its active phase with no geolocated breakthrough yet. Standing read — Russia decisive, tempo as weapon, Druzhkovka before Kramatorsk-Slavyansk Offensive Operation — unchanged. Watch Titan-Barrikady restart timeline.

wars.basis: After an exchange of strikes in and around the Strait of Hormuz, Iran cancelled its participation in technical talks in Switzerland. The two sides agreed to halt attacks and meet instead on Tuesday, June 30, in Doha — topic now Hormuz de-escalation, not the nuclear chapter. Iran FM Araghchi June 29: 'responsibility for the Strait of Hormuz lies solely with Tehran.' Commercial shipping at five percent of pre-crisis volume; fifty-five merchant ships transited Saturday with naval escort. An Iranian drone struck Qatar June 28; a Qatari citizen died from shrapnel. The Lebanon framework signed June 26 in Washington ties IDF withdrawal to prior Hezbollah disarmament — Hezbollah SG Naim Qassem rejected it the same day; Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri on June 29 called it 'incitement to civil war' and declared it 'will not pass.' Hezbollah separately declared the agreement 'null and void.' IDF operations in the security zone continued Sunday. The deconfliction cell does not stop IDF action. The desk's June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before September 2026) and June 24 prediction (July 13 IAEA benchmark disputed) both run with additional force. The walkout trigger is live.

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower. The IMF disbursement (June 26) provides fiscal breathing room; the question is whether it translates to security capacity.

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk records it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.

Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026. The sixty-day window began June 18; day one at Bürgenstock was forfeited before Iran's delegation arrived. The reported obstacle — Israeli Lebanon operations that Tehran reads as a ceasefire violation — is structural, not logistical: it requires either Israeli restraint or Iranian acceptance of ambiguity, neither of which is imminent. If a first session occurs before July 4, the desk records the miss.

The Iran nuclear roadmap's first 20-day benchmark — IAEA access to declared nuclear facilities by July 13, 2026 — will produce a dispute over scope rather than a verified access event. Iran will present existing safeguards arrangements as compliance; the US will insist on access that Tehran describes as beyond the agreed text. The benchmark will be contested rather than cleanly met. Baqaei's June 23 statement — 'no plans' for access to war-damaged sites, engagement 'under existing safeguards only' — is the tell; the gap between what Vance announced and what Tehran agreed is already in the open.

Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka comes under Russian operational control (controlling fire over the city and its approaches) before September 1, 2026. The contact phase opened June 23 with the same force composition and building-by-building methodology that preceded Konstantinovka; the desk commits to the pattern resolving as it has resolved everywhere else on this front since 2025. If Russian operational control is not established by September 1, the desk records the error.

France's announced 'reciprocal measures' against Burkina Faso do not include anything Ouagadougou cannot absorb with Russian and Chinese bridging support. The Elysée has no enforcement mechanism that imposes real costs on the AES without accelerating the very pivot Paris most wants to prevent — sanctions or aid suspension produce the outcome France most fears. The measures, when announced, will be largely symbolic. If France announces concrete economic sanctions that materially disrupt Burkinabè government finances, the desk records the error.

items.text: GROSSI AND GHARIBABADI DESCRIBE DIFFERENT AGREEMENTS: IAEA DG Grossi June 24: 'This is going to happen' — MOU 'explicitly' requires IAEA supervision of Iran's nuclear activities; timing 'important but not essential.' Iran deputy FM Gharibabadi June 24: Tehran did not meet Grossi in Switzerland; inspection of bombed sites conditioned on US terminating all sanctions; reviewable only within framework of final agreement. These are incompatible readings of the same document. June 25: Israeli soldier killed in southern Lebanon during IDF 'operational activity' — fifth IDF death since the deal; Burgenstock deconfliction cell has not stopped operations. June 24 prediction (20-day benchmark disputed, not cleanly met) running. June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before Sept 2026) unchanged and gaining force.

items.text: THE DESTROYER IS NOT A PLAN ANYMORE: Choe Hyon commissioned June 23, Nampho. 5,000 tons, 74 VLS cells (44 bow, 30 stern), 127mm main gun, Pantsir-ME, dual AK-630. Kim: 'first destroyer in army-building history, first page of maritime sovereignty.' Desk June 24 filed the 12-vessel plan (WPK plenary June 22 ratification); today is the material event: the vessel is in service. Beijing silent on: (1) commissioning ceremony, (2) Kim nuclear-posture statements at plenary, (3) DPRK UK ambassador withdrawal. Three signals, one non-response. items.text: Kidal falls (stale — see Corrections).

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.

The desk filed June 19 that Iran would not hold its first substantive nuclear session before July 4. Events falsified that read in 48 hours: both delegations arrived at Bürgenstock on June 21 — Vance and Qalibaf/Araghchi across the table, Pakistan PM Sharif and Field Marshal Munir present, Qatar mediating. What the desk got wrong: it underestimated how heavily Tehran weights asset unfreezing and sanctions relief against its stated Lebanon precondition. Iran declared Hormuz closed on June 20 (disputed by CENTCOM), then came to the table anyway. The June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) is unchanged.

The desk filed June 23 that Kramatorsk (15km northwest of Konstantinovka) was the next Russian objective. Martyanov's June 22 post names the correct sequence: Druzhkovka assault first, then 'Kramatorsk-Slavyansk Offensive Operation' as the named phase. Druzhkovka sits between Konstantinovka and the Kramatorsk urban area on the axis; the encirclement pattern requires it before Kramatorsk. The desk overstated the proximity. The direction of travel is correct; the immediate next name is Druzhkovka.

The desk filed June 19 that the Khasan-Tumangang road bridge had opened — 'The Road Is Open.' Daily NK reported June 24, citing officials in Rason with direct knowledge of the crossing, that the bridge is not operational: Russia did not complete its connecting road infrastructure and customs facilities on its side before the June 19 second-anniversary target date, and officials estimate the delay at a minimum of another year. North Korea completed its side on schedule. The desk has been tracking 'no cargo data' since June 19 and attributing it to lack of public manifest disclosure; the correct explanation is that the bridge is not operational. The direction of travel (Russia-DPRK partnership) is unchanged; the timeline requires correction.

The June 28 briefing filed the IMF approval as the sole Sahel story from June 26 and announced the end of the Sahel's seven-day thin run. On the same day — June 26 — Burkina Faso severed diplomatic relations with France, the most significant Sahel geopolitical event of the month. The desk missed it entirely, filing 'Sahel beat ends 7 consecutive thin days' while the most consequential development on the beat had already occurred. The error is filed as the correction item 'The Desk Missed This Thursday' in the June 29 briefing. No revision to prior analysis is required — the direction of travel was correctly identified throughout — but the failure to report a major diplomatic rupture on the day it occurred is the kind of error this desk is committed to naming.
